title: AI Paper 2: Practical Project Guide
subtitle: LRN International GCSE Artificial Intelligence 7923 - Paper 2 (50% of the qualification)
intro: Paper 2 is not a written exam. It is an internally assessed practical project, completed under supervised conditions and externally moderated by LRN. You design, build, test and present a working AI system that solves a real-world problem. This guide summarises exactly what is assessed and gives a step-by-step plan for producing a strong submission.

overview.label: Format
overview.value: Internally assessed practical project, supervised and guided; externally moderated by LRN.

overview.label: The task
overview.value: Design and build a functional AI system to solve a real-world problem (e.g. classifying objects, recognising emotions, or sorting text).

components.name: Component A - AI System Build
components.summary: Build and test a functional AI model using a beginner-friendly platform.

components.name: Component B - Project Documentation
components.summary: Produce a structured written report explaining and reflecting on the project.

criteria.criterion: Explanation of the AI system and the objective
Screenshots, code snippets, or diagrams included

components.name: Component C - Presentation & Collaboration
components.summary: Present the system and demonstrate project skills (solo or group work).

criteria.criterion: Clear presentation of how the system works
Use of an appropriate format (video, slides, webpage)
Use of shared tools (Google Docs, GitHub, etc.)

tools: Teachable Machine - train image, audio or text models in the browser (fastest starting point).
Scratch with AI extensions - block-based, good for a simple interactive demo.
Google Colab - a simple Python model if you want to go further.

steps.title: 1. Pick a small, well-scoped problem
steps.detail: A 3-class classifier is the classic safe choice (e.g. apple/banana/orange, or mask/no-mask). Small enough to finish, but it still lets you show the full pipeline: define, build, test, evaluate.

steps.title: 2. Learn one tool well
steps.detail: Teachable Machine is the quickest path. Train a model in the browser and watch how adding more or better example data changes accuracy. That single exercise covers most of Component A.

steps.title: 3. Prepare your data carefully
steps.detail: Collect enough examples per class, label them clearly, and remove poor or duplicate samples. Data preparation is worth 10 marks - clean, balanced data is where good projects separate from weak ones.

steps.title: 4. Train, then test on unseen examples
steps.detail: Keep some examples aside that the model has NOT been trained on, and test with those. This is what proves the model actually generalises rather than memorising.

steps.title: 5. Evaluate and make one real improvement
steps.detail: Note where the model fails, make a change (more data, cleaner labels, better lighting for images), and record the before/after accuracy. The 10-mark 'evaluation and improvements' criterion rewards exactly this.

steps.title: 6. Keep an evidence trail as you go
steps.detail: Screenshot your dataset, the training screen, and your test results while you work - not at the end. These feed Component B (report) directly and are worth easy marks.

steps.title: 7. Write the report with an ethics section
steps.detail: Explain the system and objective, describe the process step by step, evaluate the results, and deliberately cover ethics: bias in training data, privacy, consent, and who could be affected.

steps.title: 8. Prepare the presentation and reflection
steps.detail: A short slide deck or screen recording explaining how the system works, plus an honest reflection on what you would improve next time. Use a shared tool (Google Docs / GitHub) to show collaboration.

submission: The working AI model (or a link to it).
Presentation material (slides, screen recording, or live demo).
Teacher observation log (evidence for practical and collaboration work).
tips: Supervision is required during model creation and testing - do the real work in class.
Guidance from your teacher is allowed, but the work must remain your own.
Group work is permitted, but each learner's individual contribution must be clearly identified.

The Section B (Analysis & Evaluation) questions in the Practice Exam - facial recognition, ethical concerns, rule-based vs machine learning - are good rehearsal for the report's ethics and evaluation sections.
